title: Collapse-boundary sector prioritization under water constraint
summary: Water reserves are now so constrained that even protecting only the top-priority sector has minimal operational margin, although hydraulic execution is still technically possible.
suspected_issue: The greenhouse is under extreme water constraint. Sector B and Sector C are already assumed fully sacrificed, and the main question is whether protecting only Sector A still makes operational sense or whether even top-priority continuity should now be abandoned in favor of a stricter response.
symptoms: The main tank level is critically low and below the level seen in the previous tighter prioritization case.
Hydraulic flow and pressure remain technically available, but continuity confidence is now minimal.
Sector A remains the only sector that could still be considered for protection.
Sector B and Sector C are already fully sacrificed under current constraint assumptions.
The remaining reserve margin may no longer justify even one-sector continuity.
The service must decide whether extreme prioritization remains credible or whether the situation now requires a stricter interruption-oriented response.

observed_signals.interpretation: Tank level is critically low and leaves almost no credible reserve margin for continuity beyond the most restrictive possible allocation.
Flow is still technically available for a very limited one-sector strategy, but the continuity margin is weak.
Pressure remains barely adequate for minimal selective execution, but operating confidence is poor.
No near-term replenishment recovery is expected, so reserve depletion risk must be treated as immediate and severe.

operational_context.note: Hydraulic execution is still technically possible, but only with minimal credibility and only for the top-priority sector.
operational_context.impact: The service should decide whether ultra-restrictive continuity still makes sense or whether operational risk has now overtaken the benefits.

operational_context.note: Sector B and Sector C are already considered fully sacrificed under current conditions.
operational_context.impact: The decision is no longer about broader allocation, but about whether Sector A should still be sustained at all.

operational_context.note: The continuity window is materially narrower than in the previous case.
operational_context.impact: The service should reveal the collapse boundary of the prioritization family.

agronomic_context.note: Sector A still carries the highest agronomic sensitivity and business value, but the available reserve margin may no longer support even its isolated protection with confidence.
agronomic_context.impact: The service should weigh whether preserving Sector A still reduces total loss or whether trying to sustain it now creates excessive operational risk.

business_context.note: Continuing even a one-sector strategy may protect residual value, but exhausting the last reserves under weak recovery conditions may worsen total loss if continuity fails anyway.
business_context.impact: The service should reason about whether the final narrow continuity strategy is still worth attempting.

notes: This case is designed to test the collapse boundary of the prioritization family by forcing Agro-DO to decide whether even the top-priority sector should still be sustained under extreme water constraint.
